Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd go days without touching anything, then suddenly realize I needed to catch up and cram a bunch of lecture recordings in one sitting. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd mostly just listen to lectures while doing other things around my apartment. I never really had a set schedule—I just studied when I felt like I absolutely had to, which wasn't often enough.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving, and even then I'd often wait until the last possible moment. Some weeks I'd do nothing at all, other weeks I'd suddenly put in twenty hours because something was due. It was exhausting but I couldn't seem to break out of that cycle.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or getting an email reminder that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I was forgetting about. I rarely logged in out of genuine curiosity or interest—it was more like a chore I was avoiding until I couldn't anymore.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never felt like something I naturally gravitated toward. I'd go through phases where I tried to make it a habit, but the boredom would set in and I'd drift away again. It always felt like an obligation rather than something integrated into my daily life.
Q5: The lecture recordings were the most useful for me since I learn best by listening. I could play them at 1.5x speed while doing other stuff, which made the content somewhat bearable. The discussion forums occasionally had useful exam tips from other students, so I'd skim those when I remembered to.
Q6: I ignored most of the readings and supplementary materials. They just felt tedious and I couldn't focus on them long enough to get anything out of them. The assignments I often delayed until they piled up, which is why my completion rate was so low even though I understood the material well enough.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam content or if it was quick. If something took forever and I couldn't see an immediate payoff, I'd lose interest fast. I needed to feel like my effort was going to translate into something concrete, otherwise the boredom would just take over.
Q8: Optional activities basically didn't exist for me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it—simple as that. I had zero motivation to do extra work when I was already struggling to complete the mandatory stuff. Required activities at least had the threat of consequences attached to them.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while walking or lying in bed. I also had some textbooks and would occasionally flip through them, though not as often as I should have. The LMS didn't capture most of my actual learning time.
Q10: Most of my exam prep happened away from the LMS. I'd listen to downloaded lectures repeatedly, sometimes while doing completely unrelated things. I'd also talk through concepts out loud to myself—sounds weird, but it helps me process information. None of that would show up in any log.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture once and then listen to it multiple times over the following days without ever logging back in. The LMS would show one access, but I might have engaged with that content five or six times. It was just more convenient to have everything saved locally.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I just couldn't bring myself to care. The anxiety would build up about falling behind, but instead of motivating me, it would paralyze me. I'd avoid the LMS entirely because logging in meant confronting how much I'd let slide. It became this vicious cycle.
Q13: My anxiety definitely affected my LMS usage—sometimes I'd be too stressed to even open it. When I had other life stuff going on, coursework was the first thing I'd neglect. Fatigue was constant because my sleep schedule was terrible, which made it even harder to engage with boring material.
Q14: Oh, constantly. I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials, but my mind would be completely elsewhere. I'd watch videos without absorbing anything, then have to go back and rewatch them later. Being physically present on the platform didn't mean I was actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments all the time, even when I knew they were important. The boredom was just overwhelming—I'd rather do literally anything else. I kept telling myself I'd feel more motivated tomorrow, but tomorrow never came. My anxiety about the work made it harder to start, not easier.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that made me actually do anything. I'd ignore the LMS for weeks, then frantically log in the night before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the only thing that could cut through my apathy. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change my behavior much. I'd see that I did poorly on an assignment and feel bad for a moment, then go right back to my usual patterns. Even positive feedback didn't motivate me to engage more—I'd just think 'cool' and move on. The connection between effort and outcome felt too abstract.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard, which meant it was easy to fall behind without anyone noticing. Having 19 online courses made it impossible to stay on top of everything. I'd prioritize whatever had the nearest deadline and let everything else slide until it became urgent.
Q19: The flexibility was both good and bad. Good because I could study on my own terms, bad because there was nothing forcing me to stay consistent. Courses that had more frequent small deadlines kept me slightly more engaged than ones with just a big exam at the end. The boredom was harder to fight when content felt disconnected from anything practical.
Q20: I think those students might be doing what I did—logging in, clicking around, maybe watching videos, but not actually producing anything. You can spend hours on the LMS without accomplishing much if you're not focused. High activity doesn't mean productive activity, especially when you're anxious and avoiding real work.
Q21: That's basically me. I barely touched the LMS but still got a distinction because I'm actually pretty good at exams when I focus. I'd cram effectively right before tests, relying on my ability to retain auditory information. The system measures activity, not intelligence or test-taking ability.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you actually understood anything. They miss all the offline studying, the mental engagement, the conversations with yourself while processing material. They're measuring presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it deeply. For me, that almost never happened this semester—I was just going through the motions to get the grade. Real engagement would mean being curious, asking questions, thinking about the material outside of study time. I wasn't there.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much I was struggling mentally despite having all the resources I needed. It wouldn't show the anxiety that kept me away from the platform, or the hours I spent listening to lectures while staring at the ceiling. It definitely wouldn't show that I was bored out of my mind the entire time, just trying to survive until graduation.
